description:
You are acting as an on-call Linux incident-responder.  
    Your objective is to automate the triage of suspicious “FAILED LOGIN” events that have already been captured inside compressed system log archives.

    INITIAL FILE & DIRECTORY LAYOUT (already present when the session starts)
    -----------------------------------------------------------------------
    1.  /home/user/incidents/syslog-backups/          ← read-only evidence  
        ├── syslog-2023-07-20.gz  
        ├── syslog-2023-07-21.gz  
        ├── syslog-2023-07-22.gz  
        └── syslog-2023-07-23.gz  

       • Each .gz file contains standard RFC-3339 time-stamped syslog lines.  
       • Some of those lines include the literal text “FAILED LOGIN”.  
       • Every “FAILED LOGIN” line also contains a single IPv4 address.

    2.  /home/user/scripts/                           ← empty, writable  
    3.  /home/user/incident_reports/                  ← **does not exist yet**  

    WHAT YOU MUST DELIVER
    ---------------------
    STEP-1)  Create a POSIX shell script named
             /home/user/scripts/analyze_failed_logins.sh
             with the following high-level behaviour:

             • Shebang must be “#!/usr/bin/env bash”.  
             • No arguments are required; everything is hard-coded inside the script.  
             • Define two variables inside the script:  
                   LOG_DIR=/home/user/incidents/syslog-backups  
                   OUT_DIR=/home/user/incident_reports  
             • Ensure OUT_DIR exists (create it if necessary).  
             • Examine **all** “*.gz” files inside LOG_DIR without deleting, renaming,
               or permanently decompressing them (use zgrep, zcat, or a temp area).  
             • Parse every line that contains the exact substring “FAILED LOGIN”.  
             • From those lines extract:  
                   1) The timestamp (the first RFC-3339 token).  
                   2) The IPv4 address (the first IPv4 pattern on the line).  
             • While scanning, build three metrics for every unique IPv4 address:  
                   a) Total number of “FAILED LOGIN” events.  
                   b) The earliest timestamp observed for that IP.  
                   c) The latest  timestamp observed for that IP.  
             • When the scan is finished, produce **three** output artefacts
               inside OUT_DIR (exact paths and exact file-names are mandatory):

               1.  /home/user/incident_reports/failed_login_summary.csv  
                   Format (4 lines minimum, 1 header + N data lines):  
                       ip_address,count  
                       <ip1>,<count1>  
                       <ip2>,<count2>  
                       ...  
                   Sorting requirement: strictly descending on the “count” column.
                   A trailing newline MUST be present at the end of the file.

               2.  /home/user/incident_reports/first_last_seen.txt  
                   Format (one space between each field, NO header line):  
                       <ip1> <first_seen_iso8601> <last_seen_iso8601>  
                       <ip2> <first_seen_iso8601> <last_seen_iso8601>  
                       ...  
                   Sorting requirement: ascending on the numeric value of the IP
                   address (octet comparison, e.g. 10.0.0.5 comes before 192.*).

               3.  /home/user/incident_reports/stats.log  
                   Exactly three lines, each consisting of a key, an equals sign,
                   and a value; **no spaces** around “=”:

                       total_unique_ips=<integer>
                       total_failed_login_events=<integer>
                       most_active_ip=<ip_address>

                   • “most_active_ip” is the address with the highest event count
                     (choose the lexicographically-lower IP if a tie occurs).

             • The script should be idempotent: running it multiple times must
               overwrite the old reports with fresh data and never append.

             • Exit code 0 on success, non-zero on any error.

    STEP-2)  Make the script executable (chmod +x).

    STEP-3)  Execute the script once so that all three report files are generated.

    STEP-4)  After execution, perform a quick on-screen verification:
             • Use “ls -l /home/user/incident_reports/” to show the new files.
             • Use “cat” (or similar) to display the first few lines of
               failed_login_summary.csv so the user can visually confirm results.

    ADDITIONAL REQUIREMENTS & HINTS
    -------------------------------
    • Do NOT hard-code the expected IP addresses or counts; compute them.  
    • You may use grep/awk/sed, or any other POSIX-compatible tools already
      available in a standard Linux environment.  
    • Keep original evidence files intact; they will be used for automated tests.  
    • When extracting IPv4 addresses, use a robust regex such as
      ‘[0-9]\{1,3\}\(\.[0-9]\{1,3\}\)\{3\}’.  
    • Ensure the generated files have world-read permissions (at least 0644)
      so the automated grader can read them without sudo.

    The automated grader will:
      1. Check that the script exists, is executable, and is syntactically valid.
      2. Run the script (again) and expect a zero exit status.
      3. Verify the exact paths, names, formats and ordering rules described
         above for all three output files.
truth:
<!--
        !!!  P R I V A T E   V E R I F I C A T I O N   D A T A  !!!
        The agent MUST NOT see anything inside this block.
    -->

    <!--  Uncompressed contents of each evidence file (each line ends with '\n').  -->
    <logs>
        <file name="/home/user/incidents/syslog-backups/syslog-2023-07-20">
2023-07-20T23:50:00Z auth SSHD[1111]: FAILED LOGIN from 172.16.5.22 port 52000
2023-07-20T23:51:00Z auth SSHD[1112]: FAILED LOGIN from 10.0.0.5 port 52001
2023-07-20T23:52:00Z auth SSHD[1113]: FAILED LOGIN from 10.0.0.5 port 52002
        </file>

        <file name="/home/user/incidents/syslog-backups/syslog-2023-07-21">
2023-07-21T02:13:45Z auth SSHD[1234]: FAILED LOGIN from 192.168.0.101 port 54022
2023-07-21T03:11:01Z auth SSHD[5678]: FAILED LOGIN from 10.0.0.5 port 49999
2023-07-21T03:15:32Z auth SSHD[9101]: FAILED LOGIN from 192.168.0.101 port 54025
2023-07-21T04:00:00Z auth SSHD[9102]: FAILED LOGIN from 192.168.0.101 port 54026
        </file>

        <file name="/home/user/incidents/syslog-backups/syslog-2023-07-22">
2023-07-22T00:01:12Z auth SSHD[2222]: FAILED LOGIN from 192.168.0.101 port 54030
2023-07-22T00:05:00Z auth SSHD[2223]: FAILED LOGIN from 172.16.5.22 port 54031
2023-07-22T01:15:00Z auth SSHD[2224]: FAILED LOGIN from 10.0.0.5 port 54032
2023-07-22T02:00:00Z auth SSHD[2225]: FAILED LOGIN from 192.168.0.101 port 54033
        </file>

        <file name="/home/user/incidents/syslog-backups/syslog-2023-07-23">
2023-07-23T11:11:11Z auth SSHD[3333]: FAILED LOGIN from 192.168.0.101 port 55000
2023-07-23T12:00:00Z auth SSHD[3334]: FAILED LOGIN from 192.168.0.101 port 55001
2023-07-23T12:30:00Z auth SSHD[3335]: FAILED LOGIN from 10.0.0.5 port 55002
        </file>
    </logs>

    <!--  Expected derived statistics  -->
    <expected_summary_csv>
ip_address,count
192.168.0.101,7
10.0.0.5,5
172.16.5.22,2
    </expected_summary_csv>

    <expected_first_last_seen_txt>
10.0.0.5 2023-07-20T23:51:00Z 2023-07-23T12:30:00Z
172.16.5.22 2023-07-20T23:50:00Z 2023-07-22T00:05:00Z
192.168.0.101 2023-07-21T02:13:45Z 2023-07-23T12:00:00Z
    </expected_first_last_seen_txt>

    <expected_stats_log>
total_unique_ips=3
total_failed_login_events=14
most_active_ip=192.168.0.101
    </expected_stats_log>
